# Hitbox

> Async caching framework for Rust

Hitbox is an open-source cache orchestrator for Rust. It separates caching logic from business logic, providing transparent caching for HTTP services (via Tower middleware) and for individual functions (via a `#[cached]` macro). It works with pluggable storage backends and handles coordination concerns like dogpile prevention, stale-while-revalidate, and multi-tier cache composition.

## Architecture

Hitbox is modular — pick only the crates you need:

| Crate | Role |
|-------|------|
| `hitbox` | Core: config, policies, predicate traits |
| `hitbox-tower` | `Cache` layer for Tower/Axum |
| `hitbox-http` | HTTP-aware predicates and extractors |
| `hitbox-moka` | In-memory backend (Moka — lock-free, sub-microsecond) |
| `hitbox-redis` | Redis backend for distributed caching |

## Key concepts

### Backends
Storage engines that implement the `Backend` trait. Hitbox ships with Moka (in-memory) and Redis backends. Backends can be composed into L1/L2 hierarchies — for example, local Moka in front of remote Redis.

### Predicates
Rules that decide whether a request or response is cacheable. Two kinds:
- **Request predicates** — evaluated before checking cache ("should we even look?")
- **Response predicates** — evaluated after handler runs ("should we store this?")

Predicates compose with `.and()`, `.or()`, and `.not()`.

### Extractors
Define which parts of the request form the cache key. Without an extractor, all requests to the same path share one cache entry. Extractors can pull from:
- Query parameters (`.query("page")`)
- Path segments (`.path("/products/{id}")`)
- Headers (`.header("authorization")`) with optional transforms (hash, lowercase, regex extraction)

### Policies
Control TTL, stale-while-revalidate windows, and concurrency:
- `ttl` — time before entry expires
- `stale` — window after TTL where stale data is served while refreshing in background
- Dogpile prevention — built-in, one request refreshes while others subscribe to broadcast

## Links

- Blog: https://blog.hitbox.rs
- GitHub: https://github.com/hit-box/hitbox
- Docs: https://docs.rs/hitbox
- Crates.io: https://crates.io/crates/hitbox
- Examples: https://github.com/hit-box/examples

---

## Blog articles

### Add Response Caching to Axum in 10 Minutes
URL: https://blog.hitbox.rs/blog/axum-caching-in-10-minutes/
Date: 2026-02-16
Tags: tutorial, axum, caching

A step-by-step guide to adding transparent HTTP response caching to an Axum application using Hitbox. Covers:
- Setting up MokaBackend with a 60-second TTL
- Adding a Cache Tower layer to routes
- Response predicates to only cache 200 OK (avoiding cached errors)
- Query parameter extractors for pagination-aware cache keys
- Path extractors for per-resource caching
- Header extractors with SHA256 hashing for auth-aware caching
- Cache-Control: no-cache support for client-driven cache bypass
- Full runnable example at https://github.com/hit-box/examples/tree/main/axum-caching-in-10-minutes

### Should I Use Moka or Hitbox?
URL: https://blog.hitbox.rs/blog/moka-or-hitbox/
Date: 2026-02-28
Tags: moka, caching

Overview of the Rust caching ecosystem. Explains the difference between cache storage engines (Moka, Redis, lru_cache) and cache orchestrators (Hitbox). Covers:
- The "cache storage trap" — manual cache-aside pattern with boilerplate in every handler
- Dogpile effect under high traffic and solutions (locking, semaphore, broadcast)
- Stale-while-revalidate with split TTL phases
- L1/L2 distributed caching (Moka + Redis)
- How Hitbox provides all of these as built-in, configurable features
- Conclusion: Moka and Hitbox solve different problems and work together
